item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's exactly what I wanted to begin with, but people didn't like it enough

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's DARE… wait, I meant there (damn you Shawn Ryder).

Basically, I feel dimensional fuckery regarding a single room is better done with Xyank's office, and that paradoxically this would be better if it stuck to a simple "room that decided to tell regular passage of time to screw itself", no need for interdimensional people storage of weirdly functioning "openings".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I removed the note at the end because I feel it adds too much in the way of explanation and thus kills some of the mystery.

It's still evident if you read this, though

item.title: Like it
item.content:encoded: Super-duper WEIRD. Me likey :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the clarification. For the record:

"suddenly it's gone even though it's still there"

I liked this and thought it was more than enough to hold my interest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Conceptually I agree with you—-I wanted it to just be the "suddenly it's gone even though it's still there" but no one liked it. The rest of the stuff I didn't just shit out but put in deliberately, making use of concepts I had for something called "Limited-use Door", so basically I got to merge the two together and I rather prefer it like that.

You must've misunderstood too; there are no 'tenants', that's just the word being used by the figures indicating a severe lack of English comprehension, or a very poor attempt at translation/deception. They were basically referring to the office itself as a "tenement", based on how much time Marlowe spent in the room previously.

And those aren't alternate-universe things

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was intrigued by the concept of a locked-room mystery where the room itself is the mystery. Something as simple as an office becoming inexplicably inaccessible was appealing and I was compelled to keep reading. When I came to the "mutilated bodies" bit, however, I was immediately snapped out of the reverie.

For me, the entire concept of the "tenants" is superfluous and somehow…tacky? This was concise and elegant before adding alternate universe Marlowe butchery, and its inclusion feels like a disservice to the delightful strangeness that preceded it. Null vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If there was an incredibly clear glass, you would be able to interact with it in terms of being able to see it under certain circumstances, or see light reflect off of it, or have your fingertips smush against the surface. If you somehow were able to use echolocation1, you would detect a solid surface. Simply being transparent doesn't mean the glass isn't there; it's basically detectable and interactive with all senses save for sight.

In the case of SCP-1934, there is none of that.

Footnotes
1. I don't know how echolocation works

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this weirdness. This sentence is a little confusing to me though:

Physical interaction with the open doorway produces no sign of an invisible or otherwise unidentifiable object obstructing the doorway, and attempts to breach the room from above or below have been similarly impeded.

There's no sign of an obstruction? I thought that was the whole deal. And then, if there's no sign of an obstruction, why would it say "and" attempts from above and below are "similarly impeded"? Am I misunderstanding something, or is there an issue with the sentence?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this really a 'Safe' object? Although contained, it is self-contained; Meaning that it wasn't the Foundation that did the containing, but the entity itself which made the choice (and on Foundation property, no less). Also, there are entities of unknown origin and intent that can enter and leave the room at will. They are not contained. I'm not saying they have ill intentions towards the Foundation, but they are leaving a whole mess of body parts lying around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: possibly first time in addenda of SCP-2021

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry about that. Rampant male centrism and all that. Did I miss that somewhere?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For th posterity, I rewrote all these lines you mentioned. Also even though it doesn't matter (like the characters in "Alien"), my headcannon char Marlowe is female

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's some awkward wording in there:

SCP-1934 is located in Site-19 behind a locked door specifically labeled as SCP-1934 in Corridor 6 of the seventh floor.

The office was in the process of being cleaned out when the anomaly was discovered by custodial staff as they attempted to enter the office to remove the remaining items.

No further information on SCP-1934 can be detected given the technological capabilities of the Foundation.

All sentences that could probably benefit from a rewrite. There might be more, but these jumped out at me.

Also:

Access is restricted outside of testing.

Since we can't enter the room, isn't that kind of a given? Even disregarding that, that's still kind of a no-brainer.

Anyway, none of those things bother me too much, because in the end this is some weird shit and I - like Kalinin - can almost taste Marlowe's discomfort as she sees herself mutilated over and over again. Like these entities have the room trapped in a loop and are using it to experiment on human beings. And that note is pure gold.

So that gets this a well-deserved +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Satisfyingly weird, while not being so incomprehensible as to stray into the dreaded "lolrandom" zone. Imagining how disturbing this would be to encounter for Dr. Marlowe, even though this isn't even a hostile phenomenon as such, is sort of an amusing mental image.

Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Normally, this kind of setup would end in Marlowe being stuck in there, too, screaming and trying to get out or just in some kind of stasis. And no one very few people at the Foundation deserve that kind of thing, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
if you mean the Limited-use Doors, then this is basically the end result of that attempt, but with different execution.

And yes, I think a spare set of vital organs is a good tradeoff for an old office and a filing cabinet

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like there are a bunch of little things I'm not connecting here (I forgot about Marlowe until 2021 was explicitly linked, that sort of thing), but this is really fun and I'm enjoying these skips of yours. Does this have anything to do with the doors, plural?

One thing I'd suggest is just specifying that Dr. Marlowe works for the Foundation. I wondered that at the start, since I didn't remember his name. Still, I love Foundation-based skips, and I particularly love that for once, a researcher didn't get totally screwed over by a personal anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Other entities, possibly extradimensional, would appear to have appropriated Dr. Marlowe's office to use as storage for mutilated versions of the good doctor. Once they realized that someone in the Foundation universe was there to observe them, they offered parts of at least one of the corpses in payment for the use of the room.

And they're apparently able to use something like SCP-2021 under controlled conditions, including restricting the number of times the anomaly functions (used to deliver the organs through a space only large enough for a piece of paper).

item.title: Mod Post
item.content:encoded:
StevenThePotato, contentless posts are not allowed. As the Criticism Policy states:

Your posts must contain some kind of content.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wut

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no idea what is going on. It feels like there could be something more, but I'm content with what it is. A closed door.

No vote for now.

item.title: Hello, Emporion Paraphernas! : P
item.content:encoded:
Liberated segment in reverse face. Single-use restriction. Insert appendage into reverse face. Accept liberated segment for recompense of singular tenement.

… yes. Yes, I think yes. Have I said yes? That's all I've got to say, currently. : D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This idea started out as a door that disappeared after you used it, then came with added shenanigans. Then I took out the door. It was always going to be connected to 2021 in this way
